Name: Effortlessly Invariant Rulesbooks (ERB) - Project Architecture
Description: Top-level rulebook describing the ERB project itself: rulebook-as-hub, project-scoped builds via ssotme-proxy, admin portal UX, and conformance testing across substrates.

schema.name: Name
schema.Description: Effortlessly Invariant Rulesbooks (ERB)

schema.name: Purpose
schema.Description: Host a catalog of independent Effortless projects (rulebook-examples/<project>/), each a self-contained rulebook that fully captures one domain and selects the subset of platform substrates it needs. The platform proves that a rulebook is a complete spec: given just the rulebook, any frontier LLM can answer any question about the domain or produce a faithful implementation in any language / platform.

schema.name: Architecture
schema.Description: Catalog of N independent Effortless projects under rulebook-examples/. Each project's rulebook JSON is its own hub / durable SSoT, with input spokes (Airtable, LLM edits, admin portal) and a project-chosen subset of the platform's 15 substrates as output spokes. The platform itself is one such project (effortless-platform/) describing ERB.

data.Name: Effortlessly Invariant Rulesbooks
data.Purpose: Host a catalog of independent Effortless projects under rulebook-examples/, each a self-contained rulebook that fully captures one domain and selects the subset of platform substrates it needs. A rulebook alone is sufficient for any frontier LLM to answer any question about the domain or produce a faithful implementation in any language / platform.
data.Architecture: rulebook-examples/<project>/ — N independent Effortless projects (acme-llc, star-trek, jessica-advanced, jessica-basic, customer-fullname, acme-corporation, is-everything-a-language, effortless-rulesbooks, …). Each project's rulebook JSON is its own hub / durable SSoT; each picks the subset of the platform's 15 substrates it needs (acme-llc exercises all 15; most use 3+). Builds are convergent: downstream artifacts mirror the current rulebook (additions appear, removals disappear). The platform itself (effortless-platform/) is one such project, describing ERB.

OntologyAxioms.Description: Positive-form invariants the project is built on. These are the load-bearing claims; if any one is dropped, the methodology no longer holds. FramingInvariants are mistakes that violate these axioms.

schema.name: Statement
schema.Description: The axiom in one sentence

schema.name: Why
schema.Description: What collapses if you give this up

schema.name: Implication
schema.Description: Concrete consequence for how the project is built / discussed

schema.name: Status
schema.Description: active | revising | retired

data.Statement: The rulebook JSON is the portable intermediate representation. Every other artifact is mechanically derivable from it.
data.Why: If any artifact is hand-edited authoritatively, conformance no longer follows from a single source. The 'all substrates compute identically' claim depends on one IR.
data.Implication: Generated files are disposable; the rulebook JSON is the only file that is sacred.

data.Statement: Postgres, Airtable, Excel, Python, Go, OWL, COBOL — every substrate is a peer projection of the rulebook. None is the reference.
data.Why: Privileging one substrate quietly redefines 'correct' as 'matches that substrate' instead of 'matches the rulebook', which is the actual SSoT.
data.Implication: Substrate properties (maturity, transpiler source, expressive completeness) are facts about implementations, not authority rankings.

data.Statement: The 'answer key' for a conformance run is whichever substrate the user designates as SSoT for that run — Airtable export, Excel workbook, hand-edited JSON, or a Postgres dump.
data.Why: If the answer key is fixed, the methodology only works in one deployment mode. Locality lets the same harness validate Airtable-driven, Excel-driven, or JSON-driven projects.
data.Implication: Conformance reports must name the chosen SSoT for the run; trustworthy substrates witness, they do not crown.

data.Statement: The rulebook is the hub. Input spokes (Airtable, LLM, admin portal, hand edits) write to it. Output spokes (substrates) read from it. Spokes never talk to each other.
data.Why: Direct spoke-to-spoke traffic re-introduces the n×n integration problem the rulebook was created to eliminate.
data.Implication: If you find yourself wiring a substrate to consume another substrate's output, you are violating the topology — route through the rulebook.

data.Statement: When a file or value is not where it is expected, the code fails with the exact expected path. It does not check a second location, default to a guess, or return an empty object with a warning.
data.Why: Silent fallbacks mask the bug they are working around and cause 100% conformance against the wrong data.
data.Implication: Defaults derived from the SSoT are fine. Defaults that substitute a guess are not.

data.Statement: The platform rulebook (this file) is the parent describing ERB itself. Demo rulebooks under rulebook-examples/ are children describing one business domain each. They never mix.
data.Why: Mixing platform-meta with business-data couples the wrapper to a domain, which destroys the wrapper's portability.
data.Implication: Portal config tables (AppUsers, AppNavigation, etc.) live ONLY in this file. Business tables (Customers, Episodes, etc.) live ONLY in their demo rulebook.

data.Statement: The admin portal is the app; a domain is a document the app opens. Their databases are separate (erb_admin_portal vs erb_<domain>) and the names never merge.
data.Why: Names like erb_admin_<domain> conflate the editor with the thing being edited, which leads to drift between portal state and document state.
data.Implication: Two Postgres connections at all times: one to the portal's own DB, one to the active document's DB.

data.Statement: Every build runs all transpilers, then runs every substrate's conformance test, then regenerates and opens the report.
data.Why: A build that skips the test step produces output of unknown correctness; the methodology's value is the conformance proof, not the codegen.
data.Implication: There is no 'build without testing' menu option. If a substrate has no take-test.sh, that is a bug to fix, not a case to handle.

data.Statement: The admin portal and ./start.sh --cli are peer interfaces to the same effortless.json pipeline. Every portal mutation shells out to the same `effortless` CLI command.
data.Why: Two divergent implementations of 'install a tool' or 'run a build' will drift; conformance claims become qualified ('works in CLI, fails in portal').
data.Implication: If the portal grows logic the CLI does not have, that logic moves into the CLI and the portal calls it.

data.Statement: The portal's editor Postgres DB is live; the rulebook JSON is durable. Every save writes to BOTH in the same logical transaction.
data.Why: If Postgres ever wins alone, JSON drifts and the SSoT property is violated. If JSON ever wins alone, the live editor lies about state.
data.Implication: Drop Postgres at any time and rebuild from JSON; never the other direction.

data.Statement: A rulebook alone — no accompanying source code, no documentation — is sufficient for any frontier LLM to answer any question about the domain or produce a faithful implementation of the rules in any language, platform, or context.
data.Why: This is the load-bearing portability claim. If the rulebook is not self-sufficient, the methodology degrades into documentation-plus-code where the docs lie and the code is the real spec.
data.Implication: Anything required to understand or reproduce the domain MUST live in the rulebook. If a generated artifact contains business logic not derivable from the rulebook, that logic belongs IN the rulebook, not in the substrate.

data.Statement: `effortless build` makes downstream artifacts conform to the current rulebook: additions appear, removals disappear, renames propagate. Generated artifacts are a projection of the rulebook's current state, never an accumulation of past states.
data.Why: This is the property that distinguishes ERB from a conventional code generator. Codegen accumulates (you run it, code appears, removing things from the spec doesn't remove the corresponding code). ERB conforms — the projection is total, so the rulebook stays the single source of truth in both directions.
data.Implication: Hand-edits to generated artifacts are erased on the next build by design. The rulebook is the only place to add, modify, OR remove behavior.

data.ShortName: Formula dialect is a rulebook property
data.Statement: A rulebook declares which formula dialect it uses (Excel, Airtable, …). Substrates implement the dialect's semantics; the conformance proof is 'all substrates compute identically under this rulebook's declared dialect.' The dialect is metadata on the rulebook, not a property of ERB.
data.Why: Hardcoding one dialect as 'the ERB formula language' would couple the methodology to that dialect's quirks and exclude domains that already think in another (e.g. Airtable-native teams). The conformance machinery doesn't care which dialect is in use; it only cares that every substrate's transpiler implements the same one.
data.Implication: Excel is the current default because domain experts can read it, but it is not invariant. A rulebook may declare a different dialect, and substrate transpilers must honor the declared dialect.

schema.Description: Short title for grouping / display

schema.name: Category

schema.name: CorrectFraming

data.Name: Postgres is the reference substrate
data.WrongFraming: Postgres is the reference substrate with no expressive gaps; other substrates are demonstrations with gaps relative to it.
data.CorrectFraming: Postgres is one of N peer substrates. It happens to have few expressive gaps in practice, which makes it a convenient witness — but witnessing is not the same as crowning.
data.Why: Calling Postgres 'the reference' redefines 'correct' as 'matches Postgres'. The actual SSoT is the rulebook (or the user-designated answer-key substrate for that run). Postgres being trustworthy doesn't make it privileged.
data.ExampleContext: Said by Claude when describing the repo on 2026-05-25. Caught by user in the same turn.

data.Name: The rulebook JSON is always the SSoT
data.WrongFraming: The rulebook JSON file is the single source of truth in every deployment.
data.CorrectFraming: The rulebook is the portable IR. The SSoT for a given conformance run is whichever spoke the user designates — Airtable export, Excel workbook, hand-edited JSON, etc. If Airtable is the SSoT, the rulebook JSON is downstream from it.
data.Why: Treating the JSON as universally authoritative ignores that input spokes can BE the SSoT. The framework supports several deployment modes; only one of them has the JSON as primary.
data.ExampleContext: Implicit in any phrasing like 'the JSON is the SSoT, period' without naming the deployment mode.

data.Name: IsProduction = trustworthy
data.WrongFraming: Substrates with IsProduction=true (Postgres, Excel, Entity Framework) are the trustworthy ones; the others are proof-of-concept.
data.CorrectFraming: 'Production' here means 'backed by an officially-licensed transpiler' — a fact about who built the generator, not about whether the substrate computes correctly. Trustworthiness is measured by passing the conformance tests.
data.Why: Using one column to mean both 'authorized vendor built it' AND 'we trust its output' conflates provenance with correctness. They are independent properties; conformance is what proves correctness.
data.ExampleContext: The original ExecutionSubstrates.IsProduction column collapses these two meanings.

data.Name: Project rulebook is a demo rulebook
data.WrongFraming: Portal-config tables (UserRoles, AppUsers, AppNavigation) belong in whichever rulebook is active.
data.CorrectFraming: Portal-config tables live ONLY in the project rulebook (this file). Demo rulebooks contain only their domain's business tables. Two file reads, two concerns, never merged.
data.Why: Mixing platform-meta with business-data couples the wrapper to one domain. The wrapper has to work for every demo; the demo has to travel without portal config.
data.ExampleContext: Anytime someone proposes 'just add UserRoles to acme-llc-rulebook.json so the portal works against it'.

data.Name: Admin portal is named after a domain
data.WrongFraming: Database names like erb_admin_acme_llc, erb_admin_star_trek — admin portal plus the active domain.
data.CorrectFraming: erb_admin_portal (singular) for the portal's own state; erb_<domain> for each document. The word 'admin' appears only in the portal's own DB name and never with a domain suffix.
data.Why: The naming itself proves the category error: the app is not named after the document it opens. Word's install directory is not 'Word_my_resume'.
data.ExampleContext: If you see erb_admin_<domain> in code, the code is wrong — flag it as a category-error bug, not as fact.

data.Name: Silent fallback when a path is missing
data.WrongFraming: If a rulebook file is not at the expected path, check a legacy location or return {} with a warning.
data.CorrectFraming: Fail loudly with the exact expected path. Every fallback hides a bug; there are no compatibility shims in a project this young.
data.Why: Fallbacks make conformance run against the wrong data and report 100% pass, masking the real failure. A loud failure produces a fixable stack trace.
data.ExampleContext: Any `if not os.path.exists` followed by a default that isn't derived from the SSoT.

data.Name: Defaults are fallbacks
data.WrongFraming: All `or`-defaults are fallbacks and should be removed.
data.CorrectFraming: A default computed from the SSoT (e.g. `or f"erb_{active_domain}"`) is NOT a fallback — it's the deterministically-correct value the env var would override. A fallback is one that silently substitutes a guess.
data.Why: Over-correcting on the 'no fallbacks' rule removes correct SSoT-derived defaults and forces every caller to set env vars. The test: if the default would still be correct after the env var was unset by accident, it's a default.
data.ExampleContext: DATABASE_URL with a default of `erb_{active_domain}` is fine. DATABASE_URL with a default of `postgres` is a bug.

data.Name: Build can run without testing
data.WrongFraming: Add a 'build without testing' option so users can iterate faster.
data.CorrectFraming: Build = generate + test + regenerate report + open. The conformance proof is the point of the build; codegen alone produces output of unknown correctness.
data.Why: Splitting build from test makes 'did this work' ambiguous and silently allows substrates to drift apart between formal runs.
data.ExampleContext: Any menu addition like '[B] Build only' or '[R] Regenerate report only'.

data.Name: Portal grows logic the CLI lacks
data.WrongFraming: It's easier to implement 'install a tool' directly in the portal backend than to call the effortless CLI.
data.CorrectFraming: The portal shells out to the same `effortless` CLI command the user would run by hand. Both surfaces are peer interfaces to the same pipeline.
data.Why: Two implementations of the same operation will drift; conformance claims become qualified by interface.
data.ExampleContext: Anywhere the portal backend reaches into effortless.json directly instead of invoking `effortless -install`.

data.Name: Postgres editor wins over JSON
data.WrongFraming: Persist edits to Postgres now and write them to JSON on demand (lazy sync).
data.CorrectFraming: Every save is write-through: Postgres and JSON in the same logical transaction. Postgres is the live editor; JSON is the durable SSoT.
data.Why: Lazy sync means a crash leaves the JSON behind the editor — and the JSON is the only file that travels with the repo.
data.ExampleContext: Anywhere a PATCH endpoint touches the DB but defers writing JSON.

data.Name: Swappable active domain
data.WrongFraming: The platform has one stack and a 'swappable active domain' — switch the active domain (acme-llc, star-trek, jessica-advanced, …) and the entire stack regenerates against it.
data.CorrectFraming: rulebook-examples/ is a CATALOG of N independent Effortless projects. Each project owns its own rulebook and picks the subset of the platform's 15 substrates it needs (acme-llc uses all 15; most use 3+). active-domain.txt is a convenience pointer for the orchestrator/portal — not the architecture.
data.Why: The 'swappable' framing implies one shared stack parameterized by domain. In reality each project is self-contained; the platform is a catalog, not a runtime that mounts one domain at a time. Calling them 'swappable' hides the fact that projects choose different substrate subsets and evolve independently.
data.ExampleContext: README phrasing like 'Swappable demo domains — switch the active domain and the entire stack regenerates against it.' Caught by user 2026-05-25.

data.Name: ERB is a code generator
data.WrongFraming: ERB generates code from a declarative spec — like any other code generator.
data.CorrectFraming: `effortless build` is CONVERGENT, not additive. Downstream artifacts mirror the current rulebook: additions appear, removals disappear, renames propagate. A conventional code generator only adds; ERB conforms. The rulebook is the SSoT in BOTH directions.
data.Why: Calling ERB a 'code generator' anchors readers on a category (one-way emit-and-forget) that misses the load-bearing property. The convergence is what lets the rulebook stay authoritative — if removals didn't propagate, generated code would silently outlive its spec and the SSoT claim would collapse.
data.ExampleContext: Any framing like 'multi-substrate code generation from declarative rulebooks' without the convergence property called out.

data.Name: Excel formulas are the formula language of ERB
data.WrongFraming: ERB formulas are Excel-compatible (IF, AND, OR, CONCAT, LEFT, RIGHT, …) — that's how calculated fields work in ERB.
data.CorrectFraming: ERB rulebooks declare a formula DIALECT. Excel is the current default because domain experts can read it, but Airtable dialect (or any other) is equally valid. The dialect is a per-rulebook property; the conformance claim is 'all substrates compute identically UNDER THIS RULEBOOK'S DECLARED DIALECT.'
data.Why: Hardcoding Excel as 'the ERB formula language' couples the methodology to one dialect's quirks (1-indexed strings, specific function set, etc.) and excludes teams who already think in another dialect. The dialect is metadata on the rulebook; the platform's job is to make every substrate's transpiler honor whichever dialect is declared.
data.ExampleContext: CLAUDE.md 'Key Principles' listing 'Formula semantics are Excel-compatible' as a property of ERB, or any rulebook doc that names Excel functions as if they were the language rather than the dialect.

PlatformFeatures.Description: The catalog of distinctive features the ERB platform offers. The rulebook is the formal SSoT for this list; the repo's README and per-feature README files MUST conform to these rows. Each feature has a one-line summary (the elevator pitch), a Tier (headline vs additional), a Priority (sort order within tier), and a ReadmeFilePath (where the long-form explanation lives — even if it's still a stub). IsReadmeStub is calculated so 'which READMEs are still missing' is a queryable fact, not a tribal one. Many features reference an OntologyAxiom — that's the axiom the feature operationalizes.

schema.name: FeatureId
schema.Description: Stable PK, e.g. feature-001

schema.name: Name
schema.Description: Full feature name as it appears in headings

schema.name: ShortName
schema.Description: Short identifier for cross-references (e.g. 'ADP', 'Hub-and-Spoke')

schema.name: Tier
schema.Description: headline | additional — drives the two README sections

schema.name: Priority
schema.Description: Sort order within tier (lower = higher up)

schema.name: OneLineSummary
schema.Description: The elevator pitch — one sentence, no clauses about hypotheticals

schema.name: ReadmeFilePath
schema.Description: Path to the per-feature README, relative to repo root. Hand-maintained but MUST conform to this row.

schema.name: ReadmeStubContent
schema.Description: The placeholder one-sentence body that ships in the README until it's fleshed out. Lets us hand-maintain READMEs while keeping the rulebook authoritative for the seed content.

schema.name: ReadmeLength
schema.Description: Best-known character count of the README file on disk. Populated by a tooling pass; null means 'not yet measured'. Used to compute IsReadmeStub.

schema.name: RelatedAxiomId
schema.Description: FK to OntologyAxioms.AxiomId — the axiom this feature operationalizes (if any)

schema.name: Status
schema.Description: shipped | partial | planned — current implementation status

schema.name: IsReadmeStub
schema.Description: True if the README is still a placeholder (ReadmeLength is null OR < 400). Makes 'missing READMEs' a first-class query.

data.Name: Abstract Derivative Percentage (ADP)
data.OneLineSummary: A first-class, measurable percentage of any ERB project that is derivative (mechanically rebuildable) vs. hand-written — `effortless -clean` deletes everything derivative, build restores it, and the LOC delta is the ADP.
data.ReadmeStubContent: ADP draws a bright red line between Derivative Code (rebuildable on demand) and Hand Code (would be permanently lost without backup). Measured as (LOC_before_cleanall - LOC_after_cleanall) / LOC_before_cleanall. A pure scaffold starts at ~100%; a typical ERB project lands at 60-80%.

data.Name: effortless -clean / implicit clean before build
data.OneLineSummary: `effortless -clean` removes every file whose OverwriteMode is ALWAYS, plus every NEVER-mode file that still matches the original generator output — both are Derivative Code by definition. Every build runs this implicitly first, so 'following along' is the default.
data.ReadmeStubContent: Clean is the mechanism that lets ADP be measured. OverwriteMode=ALWAYS files are always derivative; OverwriteMode=NEVER files are derivative as long as their content still matches the generator output. Once a NEVER file is customized, it crosses into Hand Code and clean leaves it alone.

data.Name: Rulebook as IR / Hub-and-Spoke Topology
data.OneLineSummary: The rulebook JSON is the hub; every input (Airtable, LLM, admin portal, hand edits) and every output (Postgres, Python, Go, Excel, OWL, …) is a spoke. Spokes never talk to each other — they all route through the rulebook, eliminating the n×n integration problem.
data.ReadmeStubContent: The topology IS the architecture: one durable IR, N peer spokes. Generated artifacts are projections of the rulebook, never accumulations.

data.Name: Convergent Builds (not Additive Codegen)
data.OneLineSummary: `effortless build` makes downstream artifacts mirror the current rulebook — additions appear, removals disappear, renames propagate. A conventional code generator only adds; ERB conforms, in both directions.
data.ReadmeStubContent: Conventional codegen is one-way: you remove a field from the spec, the generated column lingers until someone notices. ERB is two-way: removing a field from the rulebook removes it from every substrate on the next build. This is what makes the rulebook stay authoritative.

data.Name: No Privileged Substrate / Portability
data.OneLineSummary: Every substrate (Postgres, Python, Go, OWL, COBOL, Excel, English, ARM64) is a peer projection of the rulebook. Languages not yet invented can be plugged in down the road by writing one transpiler — the rest of the stack lights up unchanged.
data.ReadmeStubContent: No substrate is the reference. Trustworthiness is measured by passing the conformance tests, not by being Postgres. This is what justifies 'portable across not-yet-invented languages'.

data.Name: Conformance Testing Across Substrates
data.OneLineSummary: Every build runs every substrate's conformance test against a locally-designated answer key and shows the pass/fail matrix. Substrate agreement is the empirical receipt that the rulebook is the SSoT.
data.ReadmeStubContent: There is no 'build without testing'. Build = generate + test + regenerate report + open. If a substrate has no take-test, that is a bug to fix, not a case to handle.

data.Name: Locally-Designated SSoT
data.OneLineSummary: The 'answer key' for a conformance run is whichever spoke the user designates — Airtable export, Excel workbook, hand-edited JSON, Postgres dump. The same harness validates Airtable-driven, Excel-driven, and JSON-driven projects.
data.ReadmeStubContent: SSoT is a per-run designation, not a global property. The rulebook is the portable IR; the SSoT for a given run is whichever substrate the user nominates as the oracle.

data.Name: Portal/CLI Parity
data.OneLineSummary: The admin portal and `./start.sh --cli` are peer interfaces to the same `effortless.json` pipeline. Every portal mutation shells out to the same `effortless` CLI command, so the two surfaces cannot drift.
data.ReadmeStubContent: Two surfaces, one pipeline. If the portal grows logic the CLI doesn't have, that logic moves into the CLI and the portal calls it.

data.Name: Write-Through Invariant
data.OneLineSummary: The portal's editor Postgres DB is live; the rulebook JSON is durable. Every save writes to BOTH in the same logical transaction — drop Postgres at any time and rebuild from JSON, never the other direction.
data.ReadmeStubContent: The rulebook JSON is the only file that travels with the repo. Postgres is convenience; JSON is truth. Lazy sync is forbidden.

data.Name: Fail Loudly, Never Fall Back
data.OneLineSummary: When a file or value isn't where it's expected, the code fails with the exact expected path. No silent fallbacks. Defaults derived from the SSoT are fine; defaults that substitute a guess are not.
data.ReadmeStubContent: Silent fallbacks make conformance run against the wrong data and report 100% pass, masking the real failure. A loud failure produces a fixable stack trace.

data.Name: Rulebook Is a Complete Spec
data.OneLineSummary: A rulebook alone — no accompanying source code, no documentation — is sufficient for any frontier LLM to answer any question about the domain or produce a faithful implementation in any language.
data.ReadmeStubContent: The load-bearing portability claim. If the rulebook were not self-sufficient, the methodology would degrade into docs-plus-code where the docs lie and the code is the real spec.

data.Name: Per-Rulebook Formula Dialect
data.OneLineSummary: Each rulebook declares its formula dialect (Excel, Airtable, …). Substrates honor the declared dialect; the conformance claim is 'all substrates compute identically under THIS rulebook's declared dialect.'
data.ReadmeStubContent: Excel is the current default because domain experts can read it, but it is not invariant. Dialect is metadata on the rulebook, not a property of ERB.

data.Name: Project Rulebook ≠ Demo Rulebook
data.OneLineSummary: The platform rulebook describes ERB itself; the demo rulebooks under `rulebook-examples/` describe one business domain each. They never mix — portal config lives only in the platform rulebook; business tables live only in their demo rulebook.
data.ReadmeStubContent: Mixing platform-meta with business-data couples the wrapper to a domain, which destroys portability. Two file reads, two concerns, never merged.

data.Name: Admin Portal ≠ a Domain
data.OneLineSummary: The portal is the app (`erb_admin_portal` DB); a domain is a document the app opens (`erb_<domain>` DB). The name 'admin' never appears with a domain suffix — `erb_admin_<domain>` is a category-error red flag.
data.ReadmeStubContent: Word's install directory isn't named after any document; a .docx isn't named after Word. Two connections, two purposes, two categories.

data.Name: ssotme-proxy Transpiler Bus
data.OneLineSummary: A local HTTP server on `localhost:4242` exposes every injector as a first-class `ssotme://` transpiler route, so repo-local transpilers and officially-licensed ones look identical to the CLI.
data.ReadmeStubContent: POST /rulebook-to-python, POST /rulebook-to-postgres, … Each route runs the corresponding injector. The proxy IS the mechanism, not a wrapper.

data.Name: Self-Hosting Platform (dog-fooding)
data.OneLineSummary: The orchestration tool, admin portal, and ssotme-proxy are themselves generated from the platform rulebook in `effortless-platform/effortless-rulebook/effortless-rulebook.json` — ERB is its own first customer.
data.ReadmeStubContent: The wrapper eats its own dog food. Every feature, screen, API, and table you see in the portal exists because the platform rulebook says it does.

ExecutionSubstrates.Description: Runtime environments that execute business rules derived from the rulebook

schema.name: Name
schema.Description: Human-readable substrate name

schema.name: Technology
schema.Description: Language/platform (PostgreSQL, Python, Go, Excel, OWL, PlantUML, etc.)

schema.name: RelativePath
schema.Description: Path within repo: execution-substrates/{technology}/

schema.name: InjectorScript
schema.Description: Code generation script: inject-into-{technology}.py

schema.name: TestScript
schema.Description: Conformance test script: take-test.py

schema.name: TranspilerSource
schema.Description: licensed-effortless-tool | local-injector | external. WHO BUILT THE GENERATOR — provenance, not trustworthiness. Replaces the old IsProduction column. See FramingInvariants.framing-003.

schema.name: Maturity
schema.Description: prototype | demonstrating | reference-quality — how complete THIS substrate's implementation is. Independent of TranspilerSource.

schema.name: ExpressiveCompleteness
schema.Description: full | partial-aggregation | partial-formula | shape-only — which formula classes this substrate can faithfully evaluate. A factual property of the substrate engine.

schema.name: CanBeAnswerKey
schema.Description: Can serve as the oracle (SSoT) in a conformance run if the user designates it. True for any substrate whose output is fully witnessable. NOT an authority ranking — see ax-002.

schema.name: Determinism
schema.Description: deterministic | stochastic | externally-influenced — pure-function reproducibility class of the substrate

schema.name: RuntimeKind
schema.Description: database | spreadsheet | binary | text-emit | graph | docs — what kind of artifact this substrate produces / runs as

schema.name: Status
schema.Description: Operational status (active, proof-of-concept, deprecated). Lifecycle, not trustworthiness.

schema.name: Description
schema.Description: Purpose and capabilities

data.Name: Binary (ARM64)

data.Name: PostgreSQL

data.Name: Explain DAG
data.Description: JSON spec for calculated-field derivation tracing; embedded React visualizer. Witnesses derivation structure, not numeric output.

data.Name: Entity Framework
data.Description: Licensed transpiler: C# POCOs + EF DbContext. Targets .NET runtime; conformance via running app.

RulebookSourceSpokes.Description: Peer input spokes that can write into effortless-rulebook.json. The rulebook JSON is the durable SSoT; these are interchangeable sources.

schema.name: Kind
schema.Description: airtable | admin-portal | llm-direct | manual-json | reverse-sync

schema.name: Direction
schema.Description: input (writes into rulebook) | output (reads from rulebook) | bidirectional

schema.name: Required
schema.Description: False for all input spokes — any one is sufficient, none is required

schema.name: Authority
schema.Description: On conflict, the rulebook JSON wins; spokes are the editors, not the SSoT

data.Name: Admin Portal (Postgres editor)
data.Purpose: Local web UI; edits flow through Postgres for real-time UX, write-through to rulebook JSON on every save. Primary developer experience.
data.Authority: Rulebook JSON is durable SSoT; Postgres is the live editor only and can be dropped/rebuilt from JSON at any time.

data.Name: Airtable (airtable-to-rulebook)
data.Purpose: Official Effortless tool: pull schema + data from an Airtable base into the rulebook JSON. Optional input spoke (was the historical HEAD).
data.Authority: Rulebook JSON wins; reverse-sync is a separate spoke.

data.Name: Reverse Sync (rulebook-to-airtable)
data.Purpose: Push rulebook JSON edits back to an Airtable base for human review
data.Authority: Output only; never reads from Airtable.

data.Name: LLM Direct Edits
data.Purpose: Claude / agent edits effortless-rulebook.json directly via filesystem; admin portal reflects changes on reload.
data.Authority: Rulebook JSON is the canonical surface for LLM edits.

data.Name: Rulebook Cache (offline)
data.Purpose: Cache of previously-pulled Airtable rulebooks; used when Airtable API unavailable
data.Authority: Last-known-good copy; superseded by any newer authoritative pull.

data.Name: Manual JSON Edits
data.Purpose: Developer hand-edits effortless-rulebook.json in their editor; no special tooling required.
data.Authority: Rulebook JSON is the file under version control.

CoreDataFlows.Description: End-to-end flows from rulebook to execution and testing

schema.name: Steps
schema.Description: Pipe-delimited sequence of steps

schema.name: Triggers
schema.Description: When this flow runs

schema.name: Outputs
schema.Description: Artifacts produced

schema.name: Invariant
schema.Description: Hard rule the flow must preserve (e.g. write-through, SSoT precedence)

data.Name: Start → Admin Portal
data.Steps: User runs ./start.sh | Detect active rulebook project | Start ssotme-proxy on :4242 if not running | Start admin portal backend on :7777 (auto-init Postgres + apply migrations from rulebook) | Open browser to http://localhost:7777
data.Outputs: Live admin portal in browser, ssotme-proxy + backend processes running

data.Name: Project Build (via ssotme-proxy)
data.Steps: User triggers build in admin portal OR runs `effortless build` in active project | effortless CLI walks ProjectTranspilers in active project's effortless.json | For each transpiler with proxy URL, POST rulebook to localhost:4242 | Proxy invokes injector script | Generated files written to project's output folders
data.Triggers: Admin portal Build button, CLI `effortless build`, or post-save hook after rulebook edit

data.Name: Pull from Airtable (optional input spoke)
data.Steps: User chooses 'Pull from Airtable' in admin portal | Call airtable-to-rulebook | Write effortless-rulebook.json | Reload portal Postgres editor from JSON
data.Triggers: Admin portal action OR legacy CLI menu

data.Name: Admin Save (Write-Through Invariant)
data.Steps: Developer edits in portal | Backend writes to Postgres (live editor state) | SAME REQUEST writes to effortless-rulebook.json on disk | Backend returns success only if BOTH writes succeeded | Optional: trigger flow-002 (rebuild substrates)
data.Triggers: Any save action in admin portal (table edit, field edit, formula edit, sample data edit)
data.Outputs: Updated Postgres row, updated effortless-rulebook.json, optional substrate rebuild
data.Invariant: Postgres is the EDITOR; effortless-rulebook.json is the DURABLE SSoT. If Postgres is dropped, the rulebook reconstitutes it. If the JSON is dropped, the editor has no source of truth.

data.Name: Portal Bootstrap (auto-create DB + migrate)
data.Steps: Portal backend boots | Check if PG database 'erb_<domain>' exists | If missing, create it | Read effortless-rulebook.json | Run rulebook-to-portal-schema migration (creates tables for rulebook entities + AppUsers + AppRoles + AppNav) | Seed Postgres rows from rulebook JSON | Mark bootstrap complete
data.Triggers: First boot of admin portal for a project, OR explicit reset action
data.Outputs: Postgres database ready for editing, portal navigation populated

data.Name: Rehydrate Postgres from Rulebook JSON
data.Steps: User drops or corrupts Postgres | User runs ./start.sh or clicks Reset Editor | Backend re-runs flow-005 | Editor is back online from JSON SSoT
data.Triggers: Manual reset, DB loss, or version-control checkout to a different rulebook
data.Outputs: Postgres editor rebuilt to match rulebook JSON exactly

data.Name: Conformance Testing
data.Steps: Load test cases YAML | Run each substrate's take-test.py | Collect outputs | Compare results | Generate report | Display in admin portal Tests screen
data.Triggers: Admin portal 'Run Tests' button or CLI option V
data.Outputs: conformance-report.html, pass/fail matrix shown in portal

data.Name: Switch Active Project
data.Steps: User selects a different rulebook project in portal | Update orchestration/active-domain.txt | Reload portal pointing at new project's rulebook | flow-005 if Postgres for that project not yet bootstrapped
data.Triggers: Project switcher in admin portal nav
data.Outputs: Portal now editing the chosen project's rulebook + Postgres editor

data.Name: Clean Generated Files
data.Steps: User triggers Clean in portal Tech Tools OR CLI | Walks ProjectTranspilers | Each route invoked with clean=true | Generated outputs removed
data.Triggers: Tech Tools → Clean Build, or CLI option C

NOT a CRUD form — every role gets a UX shaped for what that person actually cares about. Also drives RLS policies that Postgres applies to portal writes.

data.Name: Viewer
data.Persona: Curious newcomer poking at the project for the first time — wants to understand what it is and what it does without touching anything.
data.Tagline: See the whole project at a glance.
data.PrimaryConcerns: what the project is, what its parts are, what makes it conformant
data.Description: Read-only walkthrough. Sees the rulebook, browses substrates, reads test results. Every page is reading; no mutation controls render.

data.Name: Author
data.Persona: Domain expert who owns the business rules — thinks in entities, fields, formulas, and example rows, not in transpilers or Postgres.
data.Tagline: The rulebook is your editor. Make it say what you mean.
data.PrimaryConcerns: entity meaning, formula correctness, sample data realism, plain-English narrative
data.Description: Authors and edits rulebook semantics: entities, fields, formulas, descriptions, sample rows. Cannot run builds or touch substrate internals — that's Ops/Developer.

data.Name: Reviewer
data.Persona: Stakeholder or auditor reading the conformance story — wants to see that the substrates agree, and where they disagree wants the reasons.
data.Tagline: Do all the substrates agree? Where don't they, and why?
data.PrimaryConcerns: conformance matrix, test pass-rate, which substrate witnessed which run, framing invariants
data.Description: Read-only with a Tests-first landing. Sees the conformance matrix, can drill into any failing cell to see input/expected/actual, and reads the Framing invariants — but cannot mutate or trigger anything.

data.Name: Ops
data.Persona: Build/release engineer keeping the pipeline green — cares about builds, spokes, freshness, and what's installed where, not about the meaning of the rules.
data.Tagline: Builds green. Spokes flowing. Tests passing.
data.PrimaryConcerns: last build status, installed transpilers, spoke pull freshness, conformance matrix health
data.Description: Triggers builds, pulls from spokes, watches the matrix. Cannot edit business rules. Tech Tools off — Ops uses the same surfaces as everyone else.

data.Name: Developer
data.Persona: Person extending the platform itself — adds substrates, writes injectors, debugs the proxy, edits the project rulebook. Has all the dials.
data.Tagline: The whole stack is yours. Don't break the SSoT.
data.PrimaryConcerns: transpiler catalog, raw SQL, proxy routes, rulebook JSON, write-through invariant
data.Description: Full admin. Edit rulebook, run builds, manage users, access Tech Tools (raw Postgres, proxy logs, rulebook JSON, reset editor). Reserved for platform maintainers.

data.Title: Home
data.Story: Land here on ./start.sh. Cards: current project, rulebook size, # substrates, last build time, last test pass-rate, which spokes are active, who you're signed in as.

data.Title: Entities
data.Story: Left: list of every entity in the active project's rulebook. Right: selected entity's fields, formulas, sample rows. Developer can add/rename/delete fields inline; viewer sees the same with controls disabled.

data.Title: Formulas
data.Story: Every calculated field across all entities. Drill in to see live DAG visualization (inputs → intermediates → output) sourced from the rulebook's explain-dag substrate. Developer can edit Excel-style formula in place; portal auto-validates against the parser.

data.Title: Relationships
data.Story: FK graph (interactive). Hover a node to highlight its inbound/outbound edges. Click an edge to see which formula or column declares it.

data.Title: Sample Data
data.Story: Spreadsheet-style view of the data block of each entity. Developer can edit cells; computed columns are read-only and show the resolved value with a hoverable derivation popover.

data.Title: Substrates
data.Story: Left: every substrate this project emits (with last-build timestamp + conformance status). Right: file tree of generated output + a preview pane for any file. Developer can trigger 'Rebuild just this substrate'.

data.Title: Add Tool
data.Story: Browse the 15+ transpilers in the catalog. Click one, choose output path, hit Install. Portal shells out to `effortless -install <proxy-url>` so the result is byte-identical to the CLI path. Tool then shows up in /substrates and is ready for the next build.

data.Title: Builds
data.Story: Chronological list of builds. Click one to see which transpilers ran, duration each, stdout/stderr per route, and which files changed. Trigger-build button is gated by CanRunBuilds.

data.Title: Tests
data.Story: Matrix: rows = test cases, columns = substrates, cells = pass/fail. Click a failing cell to see input, expected, actual, and a diff. Trigger-tests button is gated by CanRunBuilds.

data.Title: Input Spokes
data.Story: List of every configured input spoke for this project. Status (last-pulled time, last-error). Developer can trigger a pull from any spoke.

data.Title: Users
data.Story: Default dev/test users from the rulebook + roles. Developer can add new users (saved through the write-through invariant, so they end up in rulebook JSON).

data.Title: Postgres Explorer
data.Story: Raw SQL console + table browser for the editor Postgres DB. Developer-only escape hatch. Background banner reminds: rulebook JSON is the SSoT, this DB is rebuildable.

data.Title: ssotme-proxy
data.Story: Live status of localhost:4242: registered routes, recent calls (route, duration, status, response size), last error per route. Restart button.

data.Title: Project Files
data.Story: Browse the active project folder. View any file. Read-only — prevents drift from rulebook.

data.Title: Raw Rulebook JSON
data.Story: Monaco editor on the raw effortless-rulebook.json. Save goes through the write-through invariant. Useful for bulk edits.

data.Title: Reset Editor
data.Story: One-button reset: drops the editor Postgres DB and re-bootstraps from rulebook JSON. The reassurance screen — makes the JSON-as-SSoT promise tangible.

data.Title: Framing
data.Story: Left: FramingInvariants — the mistakes-to-avoid catalog. Right: the wrong framing, the correct framing, why, and the axiom it violates. Toggle to Axioms tab to see the positive-form invariants. Reviewer's favourite page; required reading before defending the methodology.

data.Title: Roles & Personas
data.Story: Left: each role with its colour-themed pill, persona, tagline. Right: the role's primary concerns, landing screen (clickable), bespoke screen overrides, and the users currently assigned to it (clickable to filter the Users page).

data.Title: Project Flavours
data.Story: Each demo rulebook classified by Flavor (crud-template, computation-heavy, aggregation-heavy, graph-ontology, meta-rulebook, tutorial-ladder), Complexity, and density metrics. Click a flavour to filter the project switcher to that flavour.

data.Title: Platform Features
data.Story: Two sections: Headline features (ADP, clean, hub-and-spoke, convergent build, substrate equivalence, conformance, local SSoT, portal/CLI parity, write-through) and Additional features (fail-loud, complete spec, dialect binding, etc.). Left list, right detail. Detail shows one-line summary, status, related axiom (clickable), README file path, IsReadmeStub badge, and the seed README content. Developer can edit summary/tier/priority/README path in place — saves write-through into rulebook JSON.

data.ImplementationHints: Hero card with project Name, Purpose, Architecture (from ProjectMetadata). Below: cards for #entities, #substrates, #spokes, last test pass-rate. Every number is a clickable link — 5 substrates → /substrates filtered to this project. No mutation buttons. The list 'What you can do here' is the rest of the page; each bullet links to the screen it describes.

Same split-detail as developer, but every input becomes a label. Field rows show name + type + description in plain prose — no datatype-jargon column. Sample data is rendered but read-only. Each FK lookup field's value is a link to that row in its parent entity.

Big top-of-page pass-rate ring (e.g. 87% green). Conformance matrix below: test cases × substrates. Cells are coloured pass/fail. Click a failing cell to navigate to /tests/<test-id>?substrate=<sub-id> (the cell's diff view). No 'run' button.

Top card: 'Where you left off' — most-recently-touched entity (link). Cards: 'Entities with no description' (link, filtered), 'Formulas with no test sample' (link), 'Lookups with broken FK targets' (link). Author needs a TODO surface. Avoid showing build/test plumbing.

Field rows render as one editable line each: '<FieldName> is a <Type> describing <Description>'. Clicking expands a focused editor for that field. Author edits formula as plain text; portal autovalidates against the parser and surfaces parse errors inline. Save runs the write-through invariant. Sample data is an editable spreadsheet.

Left list: every calculated field, grouped by entity. Right: the formula in a Monaco editor with autocompletion for sibling field names. Below the editor, the plain-English explanation pulled from rulebook-to-english output. Save triggers write-through. NOT the Explain-DAG — that's its own thing; this is text-editing.

Reviewer's headline page. Top: ring chart 'N/M tests passed across K substrates'. Matrix below. Each row is a test case; each column is a substrate. Hover a substrate header to see its TranspilerSource + ExpressiveCompleteness pills. Click a failing cell → diff view: input row, expected, actual, the formula being evaluated, the FramingInvariant most likely to explain it (if any).

Left: all FramingInvariants grouped by Category (chips). Right: the selected invariant rendered as two side-by-side cards — 'WRONG' (red border) and 'RIGHT' (green border) — with the Why beneath, and a pill linking to the violated OntologyAxiom. Tab to 'Axioms' view shows all 10 axioms one per row.

Strip the Substrate detail of any 'production' badge. Each row shows pills: TranspilerSource, Maturity, ExpressiveCompleteness, CanBeAnswerKey (✓/✗), Determinism, RuntimeKind. Reviewer's goal here is to assess WHY two substrates might disagree — surface the engineering facts, not authority.

Top banner: 'Last build: <timestamp> · <pass-rate>%'. Cards: 'Spokes (N)', 'Substrates (M, K not built)', 'Tests (X passing of Y)'. Each card click filters to that screen. Banner colour reflects last-build state (green/amber/red).

Each spoke shown with last-pulled timestamp coloured by age (green <1h, amber <1d, red >1d or 'never'). Click a spoke → its detail (recent pulls, last error). Pull button on each row + bulk-pull at the top.

SubstrateContractPhases.Description: The three-phase contract every execution substrate implements. Inject is structural (schema → SDK), Execute is functional (compute), Grade is comparison. All three are 100% domain-agnostic — they translate whatever the rulebook defines without knowing what it means.

schema.name: DomainAgnostic
schema.Description: Whether this phase's code is generic across all rulebooks. All three are true by design.

schema.name: ScriptPattern
schema.Description: Filename pattern under each execution-substrates/<technology>/ folder.

schema.name: WhyDomainAgnostic
schema.Description: Explains how this phase remains generic — i.e. what it must NOT contain.

data.Name: Inject
data.Output: Runnable substrate artifact (SDK / schema / workbook / ontology / DDL)
data.Description: Reads the rulebook schema and generates entity structures (1:1 with entities) plus computation functions (1:1 with computed columns). When the rulebook changes, re-running the injector regenerates the substrate artifact — the injector itself never changes.
data.WhyDomainAgnostic: The injector script must contain ZERO domain words (no 'language', 'workflow', 'syntax', 'customer'). It only translates generic rulebook structures into target-language constructs.

data.Name: Execute
data.Description: Reads blank-test.json, populates the generated entity structures, calls the generated Calc*() functions to fill in computed columns, emits test-answers.json.
data.WhyDomainAgnostic: The test runner does NOT know what fields mean. It just unmarshals JSON, calls the generated functions, and remarshal the result. Same main() pattern works for every rulebook.

data.Name: Grade
data.Input: test-answers.json (substrate output) + answer-key.json (from rulebook seed data)
data.Description: Field-by-field, row-by-row comparison of every substrate's test-answers.json against the canonical answer-key.json derived from the rulebook seed data. No substrate holds a privileged position; answer keys come from the rulebook, not from any one substrate.
data.WhyDomainAgnostic: The grader compares JSON structures. It has no opinion about which fields exist or what they mean — disagreement is reported field-by-field.

FuzzyGradingProviders.Description: LLM providers usable for fuzzy grading of the English substrate — the ONLY non-deterministic substrate. All other substrates execute formulas deterministically; English requires an LLM to interpret prose into computed values. Use low temperature (0.1) for repeatability.

schema.name: EnvVar
schema.Description: Environment variable holding the API key. Null for local providers.

schema.name: Determinism
schema.Description: 'non-deterministic' for all LLMs; low temperature reduces variance but does not eliminate it.

schema.name: TypicalAccuracy
schema.Description: Observed conformance on the English substrate against deterministic answer keys.

schema.name: SpeedRelativeToDeterministic
schema.Description: Order-of-magnitude slowdown vs. a deterministic substrate like Python or Postgres.

schema.name: LocalRuntime
schema.Description: True if runnable without network.

data.Name: Anthropic
data.Notes: Alternative to OpenAI. Comparable accuracy on this task.

data.Name: Ollama (local)
data.SpeedRelativeToDeterministic: 3-4 orders of magnitude slower on CPU; less on GPU
data.Notes: Runs against localhost:11434. Useful for offline / no-cost runs at the cost of accuracy.

FormulaDialects.Description: Catalog of formula dialects that a rulebook can declare. Per-rulebook formula dialect is headline feature #6: each demo rulebook names which dialect it speaks (in _meta.formulaDialect), and substrates honor that declaration. The platform rulebook does not enumerate which functions a dialect supports — that lives in each demo rulebook's own formula definitions. This catalog only registers the dialects themselves.

schema.name: Origin
schema.Description: Where the dialect's surface syntax comes from (Excel, Airtable, etc.).

schema.name: FieldRefSyntax
schema.Description: How a rulebook formula references another field.

schema.name: StringConcat
schema.Description: How strings are concatenated in this dialect.

schema.name: PrimarySubstrates
schema.Description: Substrates that have the richest support for this dialect.

schema.name: Status
schema.Description: active | experimental | deprecated

data.Name: Excel
data.Notes: Default dialect for rulebooks without an explicit declaration. Covered by IF, AND, OR, CONCAT, LOWER, UPPER, SUBSTITUTE, LEFT, RIGHT, arithmetic, comparison.

data.Name: Airtable
data.Notes: Near-superset of Excel for the functions this project uses. Airtable-specific extensions (e.g. DATETIME_FORMAT) are honored when present.

DemoNarratives.Description: Curated commit-sequence narratives that demonstrate ERB capabilities in motion: the substrates 'follow along' as the rulebook changes. Used by docs and demos to walk a viewer through what changes, what does not change, and where the cost of change actually lives.

schema.name: Order
schema.Description: Order within the narrative sequence.

schema.name: NarrativeName
schema.Description: Name of the overall narrative this step belongs to.

schema.name: RelatedDomainId
schema.Description: FK to RulebookDomains.DomainId, when this step uses a specific demo rulebook.

schema.name: ObservedCost
schema.Description: Per-substrate cost observation (e.g. 'OWL 10s, English 5min').

data.WhatHappens: All substrates pass at 100% on a 9-table workflow ontology with relationships, aggregations, and boolean derivations.
data.KeyLesson: Substrates have wildly different regeneration costs even at full conformance.

data.WhatHappens: Rename and modify elements within the same ontology — add cross-entity lookups and conditional logic. Every substrate follows along with no edits to substrate code.
data.KeyLesson: Interface changes propagate automatically. This is the polymorphism payoff.
data.ObservedCost: Cost profile unchanged from BASIC — work is in the rulebook, not in substrate code.

data.WhatHappens: Swap to an entirely different domain (e.g. star-trek media catalog). Deterministic substrates regenerate and conform. The English substrate is now 100% wrong against the new answer key until its prose is regenerated by the LLM.
data.KeyLesson: The static English content is 100% stale across domain swaps. Regenerating it costs time and money — the dominant cost lives in the slowest substrate, not in the rulebook.
data.ObservedCost: Deterministic substrates < 1s; English regeneration ~5min and ~$0.10-$1 in LLM tokens.

_meta._CMCC_Summary: Top-level rulebook describing the ERB project itself: rulebook-as-hub SSoT, ssotme-proxy (10+ substrate routes), admin portal (Viewer/Developer roles, navigation, screens, APIs, write-through invariant), AppUsers + permissions, build pipeline, conformance testing.
_meta._ProjectGoal: Demonstrate that a single declarative rulebook (effortless-rulebook.json) can generate working, conformant code in 10+ execution substrates that all produce identical results — AND that the developer experience on top of it (the admin portal) is itself driven entirely by rulebook entities (navigation, roles, screens, permissions, users).
_meta._ArchitecturalHighlight: effortless-rulebook.json is the DURABLE SSoT. Input spokes (admin portal, Airtable, LLM, manual JSON) all write into it. Output spokes (Python, Go, Postgres, Excel, OWL, PlantUML, Explain-DAG, English, COBOL, ARM64, Entity Framework, Airtable reverse-sync) all read from it. Every substrate passes the same conformance tests.
_meta._WriteThroughInvariant: The admin portal's Postgres database is the LIVE EDITOR; the rulebook JSON is the DURABLE SSOT. Every save in the portal must write to BOTH (in the same logical transaction). If Postgres is dropped, the editor rebuilds from JSON. If JSON is lost, the editor has no source of truth.
_meta._PortalCliParity: The admin portal and ./start.sh --cli are peer interfaces to the SAME effortless.json pipeline. Install Tool, Build, Clean, Test, and project switching all shell out to the same `effortless` CLI commands — so portal behaviour cannot drift from CLI behaviour.
_meta._BootstrapStory: Cold start: `git clone && ./start.sh` → start.sh boots ssotme-proxy on :4242, starts portal backend on :7777, backend auto-creates editor Postgres DB if missing and runs migrations derived from the rulebook, opens browser. Developer signs in as the default user from the rulebook's AppUsers entity. Land on Home. The whole story unfolds from one command.
_meta._DeveloperJourney: Home → pick a project (or accept active one) → Rulebook to see what the project means → Substrates to see what it generates → Builds to see how it built → Tests to verify conformance → Input Spokes to see where edits come from → Users to see who can edit. Tech Tools only for escape-hatch debugging.
_meta._ResilienceClaim: Drop the entire Postgres editor any time. ./start.sh rebuilds it from JSON. The rulebook JSON travels with the repo; the editor DB does not.
